anthropic/claude-haiku-4.5.answer: Ninth-grade students are typically enrolled in Math Essentials, Pre-Algebra, Algebra 1, CP Algebra 1, or Honors Geometry, with placement based on individual data profiles and classroom performance [#q0d52]. CP Algebra 1 includes an "AMPED" option that combines math instruction with Career & Technical Education, where students earn credit for both subjects while designing merchandise for the school store [#q0d54]. For students seeking advanced coursework, a built-in acceleration path allows progression toward AP Calculus AB by taking both CP Geometry and CP Algebra 2 during sophomore year [#q0d55]. AP Statistics is available to all students regardless of honors track placement and is recommended for junior or senior year [#q0d55]. More detailed information about secondary mathematics progressions is available on the district website and in the Clayton High School Program of Studies [#q0d55].

google/gemini-2.5-flash.answer: Clayton High School's math program aims to challenge students and equip them for success by fostering deep thinking and problem-solving skills [#q0d52]. Ninth-grade students typically enroll in Math Essentials, Pre-Algebra, Algebra 1, CP Algebra 1, or Honors Geometry, with placements determined by student data, classroom performance, and teacher recommendations [#q0d52]. CP Algebra 1 offers a contextualized learning experience, allowing students to earn both math and Career & Technical Education (CTE) credit through designing, creating, and selling merchandise for the school store [#q0d54]. Honors Geometry is for students who have mastered Algebra 1 and need a faster pace and opportunities to explore mathematical concepts [#q0d54]. Students starting with CP Algebra 1 can still access AP PreCalc AB in their senior year [#q0d54]. Enrollment in AP Statistics is not limited to honors math students and is recommended for junior or senior year [#q0d55]. An acceleration path to AP Calculus AB involves taking CP Geometry and CP Algebra 2 during sophomore year, or earning Geometry credit over the summer before junior year [#q0d55]. Further details on secondary mathematics course progressions are available on the District Website or in the CHS Program of Studies [#q0d55].

gpt-5-mini.answer: Clayton High School offers multiple ninth-grade math placements including Math Essentials, Pre-Algebra, Algebra 1, CP Algebra 1, and Honors Geometry, with alternative placements determined by IEP or Content Acceleration teams and recommendations based on student data and staff review. [#q0d52] CP Algebra 1 can be taken as an AMPED two-block course that awards one math credit and one CTE credit while covering all Algebra 1 standards and including a hands‑on entrepreneurship project. [#q0d54] Students who start in CP Algebra 1 can still reach AP Precalculus AB in senior year, and AP Statistics is recommended for juniors or seniors and is not limited to honors students. [#q0d54] The district provides an acceleration path to end in AP Calculus AB by taking CP Geometry and CP Algebra 2 in sophomore year or earning Geometry credit over the summer. [#q0d55] The district posts secondary mathematics course progressions and resources on its curriculum/STEM web pages and in the CHS Program of Studies. [#q0d55]

anthropic/claude-haiku-4.5.answer:
# RIT to Khan Academy Alignment Summary

Clayton school district has access to correlation documents that link MAP® RIT score ranges to Khan Academy practice exercises for Common Core mathematics. [#q0d03][#q0d1b] These documents cover grades 2-5 and grades 6+, with exercises organized by RIT ranges and aligned to Common Core State Standards. [#q0d03][#q0d1b] The correlation was established using 2011 NWEA norms data to approximate grade levels, which were then matched to corresponding CCSS goals. [#q0d03][#q0d1b] Khan Academy exercises provide interactive problems with instant feedback, and teachers can use these correlated exercises to inform flexible student groupings and target instruction within each student's zone of proximal development. [#q0d03][#q0d1b]

About this Document

exercises are interactive problems for students with instant feedback: 
Having these exercises correlated to RIT ranges means you can use them in conjunction with your 
flexible student groupings that are also informed by RIT score results. The exercises are also 
useful for targeting learning in each student’s zone of proximal development (Vygotsky).

NWEA intends to update these links with Khan Academy twice a year as new exercises are created [#q0d03, #q0d1b]. These resources are supplementary and not meant to replace other options; MAP/MPG data should be one of many factors in instructional decisions [#q0d06, #q0d1e].

gpt-5-mini.answer: Documents show NWEA produced MAP-to-Khan Academy correlations that map MAP RIT ranges and sub-goals to Khan Academy practice exercises for Common Core math for Grades 2–5 and Grades 6+, intended for use with flexible grouping and targeting students’ zones of proximal development. [#q0d03] The correlation used 2011 norms to approximate grade levels and then matched those to Common Core State Standards; NWEA plans to update the links twice a year as new exercises are developed. [#q0d03] The documents include detailed tables linking specific RIT ranges (for example <160, 161–178, 179–191, 192–202, 203–212, and >231) to topic-level Khan exercises and corresponding CCSS standards across domains such as Geometry, Number and Operations, and Expressions and Equations. [#q0d08] The materials note these Khan exercises are supplementary, should be one of many data points for instructional decisions, and include Terms of Use and disclaimers about NWEA’s relationship to Khan Academy and liability. [#q0d2f] The documents also give examples of how to deliver exercises to students (paste URLs, present in class, use in conferences). [#q0d1e]

# Long-Range Facilities Master Plan Summary

The School District of Clayton's Long-Range Facilities Master Plan (LRFMP) is a comprehensive strategic document outlining facility development, maintenance, and management over the next 15 years, designed to align with the District's Strategic Plan and meet current and future educational needs. [#q077f] The plan was developed through a nearly ten-month collaborative process involving building tours, steering committees, sub-committees, community forums, and surveys to identify and prioritize critical facility needs. [#q0785] Captain, Glenridge, and Meramec) and The Family Center through a feasibility study led by Perkins & Will and Paragon Architecture; [#q0778] expanding Clayton High School's academic wing for career and technical education programs and renovating the auditorium; [#q0778] and upgrading athletic facilities including turf installation at Wydown Middle and improvements at Adzick and Gay Fields. [#q0778] Construction costs range from approximately $137 million to $178 million for phased improvements, or $129 million for three new elementary schools, with preliminary estimates in 2025 dollars. [#q06ae] The superintendent framed the proposal as a $135 million investment focused on facilities where students learn and belong, called "Prop O for our schools, our community, and our future," designed as a zero tax rate increase proposal. [#q06f7]

meaningful community engagement to ensure alignment with stakeholder priorities. By continuously

lays the groundwork for achieving the district’s long-term goals.

gpt-5-mini.answer: The Long-Range Facilities Master Plan (LRFMP) is a 15-year strategic roadmap for developing, maintaining, and managing Clayton’s school facilities that aligns with the District Strategic Plan and the Profile of a Clayton Graduate. [#q077f] The plan was produced through a year-long, inclusive process led by Paragon Architecture that included building tours, facility assessments, steering and sub-committee meetings, building-level and district-wide community forums, surveys, and iterative feedback to prioritize needs. [#q0778] It identifies goals to optimize resource allocation, enhance learning environments, and align facility investments with student needs, and it includes detailed deliverables such as HVAC replacement planning, facility appraisals, cost estimations, and design standards across multiple volumes. [#q077f] Recent work under the plan focuses on modernizing elementary schools and the Family Center (including a feasibility study for renovation vs. new construction), academic enhancements at Clayton High School, and athletic facility upgrades, with order-of-magnitude project cost ranges for potential construction totaling roughly $137M–$178M and a three-new-elementary-schools scenario estimated at about $129M. [#q0778] The Board has discussed funding capacity and timing, noting current debt that will retire in 2029 and that the district could support up to $90M of bonds without a tax increase, while further feasibility and site acquisition work is required. [#q06f7]

anthropic/claude-haiku-4.5.answer: The district aims to ensure all learners feel safe and valued [#q1324], commit to equitable educational growth through personalized learning experiences [#q1324], and dedicate itself to learners' social, emotional, and physical well-being [#q1324]. The district takes an "evergreen approach" to its strategic plan, evaluating and refining it regularly based on evidence [#q1324]. The 2024-2025 budget reflects these priorities by expanding funding for safety and security services [#q13ea], investing in curriculum and technology [#q13ea], and funding social and emotional supports [#q13ea]. For facilities specifically, the Long-Range Facilities Master Plan identifies four district-wide priority themes: Safety & Security, Maintenance & HVAC, Accessibility & Inclusiveness, and Curriculum & Programming [#q07a0]. Community survey data confirmed strong consensus around improving HVAC systems and expanding specialized learning spaces for innovative curriculum [#q07a0]. All expenditures are organized around five focus areas: instruction, human resources, capital improvement, programs, and financial sustainability [#q1324]. The district uses a data dashboard as an interactive tool to allow stakeholders to monitor progress and hold the district accountable to its goals [#q1324].

google/gemini-2.5-flash.answer: The Clayton School District's strategic plan prioritizes ensuring all learners feel safe and valued, committing to their educational growth through equitable and personalized learning, and dedicating efforts to their social, emotional, and physical well-being [#q138b, #q1324, #q1cc2]. These goals are supported by specific, measurable objectives, with progress monitored through a data dashboard [#q138b, #q076b, #q1172, #q1324, #q1cc2]. The district aligns its budget and resources with these strategic goals, focusing on instruction, human resources, capital improvement, programs, and financial sustainability [#q138b, #q12ad, #q13ea, #q1420, #q1324, #q1cc2]. Recent budgets continue to expand funding for safety and security services, invest in curriculum and technology, and support social and emotional staff [#q13ea, #q1420]. Additionally, the Long-Range Facilities Master Plan is designed to align with the District's Strategic Plan by optimizing resource allocation, enhancing learning environments, and ensuring facilities meet current and future educational needs [#q077f]. Community feedback has highlighted safety and security, maintenance and HVAC, accessibility and inclusiveness, and curriculum and programming as key district-wide master plan themes [#q07a0].

anthropic/claude-haiku-4.5.answer: For theater, students can take Drama, Musical Theatre, Introduction to Technical Theatre, Introduction to Speech & Debate, and Drama courses, with specific offerings varying by grade level [#q0d63]. In music, middle school students may participate in Chorus, Concert Choir, Chamber Choir (audition required), Orchestra, and Chamber Orchestra (audition required), also with grade-level variations [#q0d5d, #q0d5e]. The district has worked on alignment between middle school and high school theater programs [#q0621]. However, the middle school performing arts program faces significant facility constraints, including a drama classroom that cannot accommodate more than 10-15 students working together, severely limited rehearsal space, inadequate storage for theater equipment, and leaks throughout the performing arts wing [#q07df]. District plans aim to collocate performing arts facilities in a central location for easier access and to create space for all-school performances [#q0729].

google/gemini-2.5-flash.answer: The Clayton School District's middle school performing arts curriculum includes Drama, Musical Theatre, Introduction to Technical Theatre, and Introduction to Speech & Debate for 6th, 7th, and 8th grades [#q0d63]. The musical theater program at the middle school has been aligned with the high school program [#q0620, #q0621, #q0622]. However, the district faces challenges with its performing arts facilities, including a drama classroom that cannot accommodate more than 10-15 students and inadequate rehearsal space for the theater department [#q07df]. Leaks are present throughout the performing arts wing, and there is a lack of storage for orchestra, band, and theater equipment [#q07df]. The current auditorium is considered too small, and its lobby and restrooms are outdated [#q07df]. The district aims to consolidate "specials" like performing arts into a central, easy-to-access location within buildings to improve transition times for students [#q0729].

anthropic/claude-haiku-4.5.answer:
# Assessment and Grading Guidelines in Clayton School District

The Clayton School District views assessment as "an integral part of the teaching and learning process" [#q117f] and uses a backwards design model where teachers identify learning outcomes first, then develop assessments, and finally design classroom activities [#q117f]. The district employs a balanced approach combining both formative and summative assessments to inform instruction, adjust teaching, determine interventions, and report student achievement [#q117f]. The district administers a comprehensive range of assessments across grade levels, including state-required tests like MAP (Missouri Assessment Program) in grades 3-8, MAP-A for students with IEPs, and end-of-course assessments in subjects like Algebra I, Biology, and English II [#q1181]. District-chosen assessments include reading measures like FastBridge and Teachers College Benchmark Assessment, math assessments like NWEA-MAP, writing assessments, and social-emotional learning surveys [#q1183, #q1187]. Assessment data are used collaboratively by teachers, administrators, and support teams to identify trends, celebrate successes, and tailor supports where needed [#q0773]. Teachers use assessment information to plan instruction for individual students and groups, make instructional decisions, and inform programming choices [#q1184, #q1185].

[#q117f | 2026-04-11]
SCHOOL DISTRICT OF CLAYTON
2022 - 2023 ASSESSMENT PLAN
Respectfully submitted to the Board of Education by
Assistant Superintendent of Teaching and Learning, Dr. Milena Garganigo

google/gemini-2.5-flash.answer: The School District of Clayton views assessment as a fundamental aspect of teaching and learning, designed around learning outcomes to inform instruction and report on progress [#q117f]. Assessments, both formative and summative, help adjust instruction, determine interventions, and track achievement [#q117f]. The district outlines its comprehensive assessment plan to communicate a unified vision and various assessment types to stakeholders [#q117f, #q1180]. Data from assessments help inform decisions for groups of students, individual students, and programming choices [#q117f, #q1180]. The district uses a range of assessments, including state-required tests like MAP Grade-Level Assessments and MAP End-of-Course Assessments for various subjects and grades [#q1181]. It also utilizes district-chosen assessments such as FastBridge aReading for screener purposes, the Common Writing Assessment, and the NWEA-MAP for math for individual and group instruction planning [#q1183, #q1184, #q1187, #q1185, #q1187]. These assessments inform decision-making, identify trends, and tailor support for students [#q0773]. Scores from assessments are categorized as Excellent, Satisfactory, Borderline, or Poor based on percentage ranges [#q07fb].

gpt-5-mini.answer: The district frames assessment as integral to teaching and learning, using a backwards-design model where teachers identify learning outcomes, develop assessments, then design instruction, and it explicitly connects assessments to informing instruction and reporting progress toward outcomes. [#q117f] The Assessment Plan describes a balanced system of formative and summative assessments (state-required and district-chosen) used to monitor progress, make instructional and programming decisions, determine interventions, and communicate results to parents and stakeholders. [#q117f] Specific assessments listed include state end-of-grade and end-of-course tests (e.g., Grade-Level Assessments and EOCs), MAP/MAP-A, WIDA, and local screeners like FastBridge, NWEA-MAP, TCBA, and writing and spelling assessments, each with stated populations, purposes, testing windows, and estimated student time. [#q1181] The plan also indicates assessment uses beyond instruction, including qualification for programs (e.g., gifted placement, Seal of Biliteracy), accountability reporting, and informational reporting to families. [#q1187] District materials report that assessment data are reviewed regularly to identify trends and guide district goals and instructional planning (e.g., MAP and local assessment results cited in the strategic plan). [#q0773]

anthropic/claude-haiku-4.5.answer: In fiscal year 2022-2023, the district spent $1,035,289 on food preparation and dispensing services, while generating $707,182 in revenue, resulting in a net loss of $328,107 [#q0960]. The program includes a school lunch program that cost $428,976 and a breakfast program that cost $36,776 in that fiscal year [#q0960]. As of 2023-2024, the district served 211 students receiving free or reduced lunch, representing 9.1% of enrollment, down from 381 students (15.6% of enrollment) in 2014 [#q0960]. This represents a 44.6% decrease in the percentage of students qualifying for free and reduced meals over the ten-year period, compared to a 5.0% decrease statewide [#q0960]. In the most recent audited financial report for fiscal year 2024-2025, food services expenses totaled $1,186,086, with charges for services generating $753,343 and operating grants providing $236,583, resulting in a net expense of $196,160 [#q1e77].

google/gemini-2.5-flash.answer: In 2025, the estimated expenses for food services for the Clayton School District are $1,186,086, with program revenues of $753,343 from charges for services and $236,583 from operating grants and contributions [#q1e77]. This results in a net expense of $196,160 for governmental activities related to food services [#q1e77]. A year prior, in 2024, food services had expenses of $1,151,905, but generated a net revenue of $5,149 due to $800,507 in charges for services and $356,547 in operating grants and contributions [#q1bef]. In 2023, the food services program showed a net revenue of $203,383, with expenses of $1,075,550 against $740,154 in charges for services and $538,779 in operating grants and contributions [#q1c59]. The number of students in the School District of Clayton receiving free and reduced lunch has decreased from 381 in 2014 to 211 in 2024, representing a 44.6% decrease in the number of students and a 41.7% decrease in overall percentage for the district during this period [#q0960]. In 2022-2023, the food service program had revenues of $707,182 and expenses of $1,035,289, resulting in a loss of $328,107 [#q0961]. In 2012-2013, the program had a loss of $19,845, with revenues of $974,785 and expenses of $994,630 [#q0961]. In 2020-2021, federal revenue for school lunch was $316,690 and for the breakfast program was $101,190 [#q1353]. For 2019-2020, the estimated federal revenue included $180,000 for school lunch and $36,000 for the breakfast program [#q12df]. The revised estimated federal revenue for the same year also showed $170,000 for school lunch and $30,000 for the breakfast program [#q12dc].

gpt-5-mini.answer: The district’s food services function had program expenses of $1,186,086 and reported charges for services of $753,343 and operating grants/contributions of $236,583 for the year ended June 30, 2025, resulting in a net (expense) of $(196,160) for food services. [#q1e77] Historical district reports show food services has operated at a loss in prior years (e.g., FY 2003 net -$19,845; FY 2013 loss -$328,107; FY 2023 loss reported with revenues $707,182 and expenses $1,035,289). [#q0961] The 2023-24 and 2022-23 audited statements previously reported food services expenditures of $1,151,905 (net $5,149) and $1,075,550 (net $203,383), respectively, with varying levels of charges for services and operating grants. [#q1bef] Federal revenue lines in district budgets and prior-year estimates include specific allocations for School Lunch and Breakfast programs (for example, $180,000 school lunch and $36,000 breakfast in 2019–2020; $316,690 school lunch and $101,190 breakfast in 2020–2021). [#q12df] The demographic study also provides detailed line-item revenue/expense breakdowns for school lunch and breakfast programs across multiple years and shows per-student food service cost trends. [#q0961]

anthropic/claude-haiku-4.5.answer:
# Clayton School District Instruction Spending

For the fiscal year ending June 30, 2025, the Clayton School District spent $35,152,855 on instruction [#q1e77]. This represents the largest category of district spending, as instructional costs constitute the largest share of district spending, which is proper and is why schools exist [#q13f7]. The proposed 2024-2025 budget allocated $37,230,074 for total instruction across all funds [#q13f7]. Comparing recent years, instruction expenses were $34,378,715 in fiscal year 2024 [#q1bef] and $30,871,858 in fiscal year 2023 [#q1c59], showing an upward trend in instructional spending over time.

google/gemini-2.5-flash.answer: The School District of Clayton spent $35,152,855 on instruction in the fiscal year ending June 30, 2025 [#q1e77]. This amount was a proposed budget and was the largest share of the District's spending for that year [#q13f7]. This is an increase from the $34,378,715 spent on instruction in the fiscal year ending June 30, 2024 [#q1bef]. The proposed budget for instruction in 2023-2024 was $35,300,552 [#q142e], which was an increase from the $30,871,858 spent in the fiscal year ending June 30, 2023 [#q1c59]. In the 2021-2022 fiscal year, instruction costs were $25,539,386 [#q1cc8], and in 2020-2021, the district spent $33,095,847 on instruction [#q1d34].

anthropic/claude-haiku-4.5.answer:
# General Fund Total Expenditures for Fiscal Year 2024

For the fiscal year ended June 30, 2024, the Clayton School District's General Fund total expenditures were $21,928,620. [#q1bf2] This represented an increase from the prior year 2022-2023 spending, as the expenditure budget for 2023-2024 was set at $75.39M across all funds, an increase of $1.17M over prior year spending estimates. [#q1426] The board approved the 2023-2024 budget, including expenditures sourced from federal grants, as presented. [#q0082] The General Fund expenditures included significant components such as instruction ($2,972,138), operation of plant ($8,434,210), and building level administration ($1,240,887). [#q1bf2]

anthropic/claude-haiku-4.5.answer:
# Clayton School District Fund Balance and Reserves Over Time

Clayton maintains a minimum fund balance target of 18 percent of its expenditure budget to cover operating expenses from July through December, when most tax revenue has not yet been received. [#q1276] As of the 2024-2025 budget year, the district's fund balance is described as being "in a strong position," with actual data from 2015-2023 showing that fund balances typically rise after tax rate increases but later flatten and decline as expenditure growth exceeds revenue growth. [#q1412] Historically, the district spent down reserves significantly between 2009 and 2018, including operating reserves of $175,217 in 2009-2010, $820,654 in 2010-2011, and $2.0 million in 2011-2012. [#q1276] However, after the passage of Proposition E, the district projected a $2.8 million operating surplus and growing fund balance to approximately 40 percent by 2021. [#q12d3] More recent budget documents from 2021-2022 indicated the district would strategically spend down reserves of $700,700 while maintaining an overall fund balance of $41.2 million. [#q1cc3]

of the expenditure budget must be maintained to prevent the necessity of borrowing money to meet District expenses during 
the fall months.

google/gemini-2.5-flash.answer: The Clayton School District sets aside money in a reserve fund to cover expenses between July and December because most tax revenue arrives in late December and early January [#q13a9, #q1346, #q1276]. An 18 percent minimum fund balance of the expenditure budget is needed to avoid borrowing money during the fall months [#q13a9, #q1346, #q12d2, #q1276]. Fund balances typically increase after a tax rate hike but then flatten and decline as expenditure growth outpaces revenue growth [#q1412]. The District has spent down operating reserves in various years: $1.7 million in 2015-2016, $2.4 million in 2016-2017, and $2.0 million in 2017-2018 [#q1276]. In 2018-2019, reserves were projected to be spent down by approximately $4.0 million [#q1276]. The successful passing of Proposition E in 2019 led to an anticipated $2.8 million operating surplus and an increase in the fund balance to about 40%, exceeding the 18% goal [#q12d2, #q12d3, #q1d9c]. For the 2022-2023 school year, the District expects to strategically spend down reserves by $700,700, resulting in an overall fund balance of $41.2 million [#q1cc3]. The Debt Service Fund's balance increased by $0.6 million, while the Capital Projects Fund's balance decreased by $0.3 million before transfers in the year ending June 30, 2025 [#q1e69].

- Clayton maintains reserves to bridge cash flow between July and December, targeting a minimum ending fund balance of 18% of annual expenditures to avoid short-term borrowing. [#q1276]  
- Following Proposition E, the district projected surpluses and growth in fund balance above the 18% target; a 2019–2020 document projected about a $2.8 million operating surplus and growing the fund balance by approximately 40%. [#q1d9c]  
- A 2020 budget document similarly projected about a $2.8 million surplus and fund balance growth to approximately 40%, exceeding the 18% goal. [#q12d3]  
- More recently, the 2024–2025 budget notes that fund balances typically rise after tax rate increases, then flatten and decline as expenditure growth outpaces revenue; it states the fund balance is currently in a strong position while expenditures will be managed to improve estimates. [#q1412]  
- In 2021–2022 planning, the district projected strategically spending down reserves by about $700,700 to an overall fund balance of $41.2 million, reflecting that some years draw on reserves for priorities and capital needs. [#q1cc3]

anthropic/claude-haiku-4.5.answer:
# Debt Service Principal and Interest Payments

As of June 30, 2025, Clayton School District has general obligation bonds outstanding of $28.6 million [#q1e9f]. For the year ending June 30, 2026, the District will pay $6.57 million in principal and $1.05 million in interest, totaling $7.62 million in debt service payments [#q1e9f]. The District's outstanding bonds include issues from 2010, 2017, and 2019, with interest rates ranging from 4.70% to 5.00% [#q1e9f]. All remaining bonds are scheduled to mature by March 1, 2029, with total principal and interest payments through that date amounting to $31.19 million [#q1e9f]. The District's legal debt margin—the amount of additional debt it may authorize under Missouri law—is approximately $206.3 million [#q1e9f].

gpt-5-mini.answer: As of the District’s 2024–2025 audited financials, annual debt service (principal and interest) scheduled for 2026 is $7,615,939 and the total remaining principal on general obligation bonds was $28,642,000 as of June 30, 2025. [#q1e9f] The 2023–2024 and 2024–2025 schedules show the District’s near-term annual debt service totals decline from $7,387,339 in 2025 to $7,615,939 in 2026 and then rise in later years as remaining principal is amortized through 2029. [#q1c17] [#q1e9f] Earlier reports show higher historical totals: the District reported $8.54 million in principal and interest for 2021–2022 and $6.72 million for 2022–2023 in its budget and audited schedules for those years. [#q1344] [#q1cf2] Detailed multi-year amortization tables in the audits list the year-by-year principal and interest amounts through final maturities in 2029, with total interest for the remaining bond issues of $2,548,628 reported as part of the 2024–2025 amortization totals. [#q1e9f]

gpt-5-mini+finance.answer: The documents list planned capital outlay or facility improvement costs by building for multiple budget years, not per-pupil expenditures; they show dollar totals allocated to each building (for example, the 2020–2021 CIP lists Building Totals such as Captain Elementary $203,000 and Glenridge Elementary $371,000) [#q12f3]. The long-range facility master plan and board presentation provide larger multi-year cost estimates by location (e.g., Clayton High School priority totals and district priority totals) but these are facility needs totals rather than per-pupil figures [#q069a]. Earlier and later annual budgets show similar line-item building-level capital investments (examples: 2019–2020 Captain Elementary Building Total $30,000; 2024–2025 Captain Elementary listed capital items like chair lift replacements $98,800) [#q1296] [#q140e].

openai/gpt-5.answer: The most recent budget excerpts break out capital outlay totals by location for 2020-2021 ($922,500 district-wide) and itemized amounts per site, but not per student rates [#q12f3]. Earlier budgets likewise show capital outlay by building for 2021-2022 and 2019-2020, without any per-pupil figures [#q1368][#q1296]. The 2024-2025 proposed capital items are also listed by building (e.g., specific projects at Captain, Glenridge, Meramec, Wydown, CHS, and the Family Center) but not as per-pupil expenditures [#q140e]. Facility master plan materials provide total facility needs by location and priorities, not spending per student [#q069a][#q0862]. Therefore, these quotes are not directly relevant to “per-pupil expenditure by building” without separate enrollment data to compute per-pupil amounts. [#q12f3][#q1368][#q1296][#q140e][#q069a][#q0862]

anthropic/claude-haiku-4.5.answer:
# Property Tax Rate Hearing and Tax Levy Summary

Clayton school district conducts annual property tax rate hearings before October 1st each year, as required by Missouri law. [#q0579] At these public hearings, the board receives community comments before setting tax rates. [#q0579] For the 2025 tax year, the district's assessed valuation reached $1.73 billion, and the board proposed residential rates down 34.5 cents and commercial rates down 20 cents compared to the prior year, due to growth in reassessment. [#q0579] The district's tax system is designed so that "when assessed valuations rise rates are forced to decrease so that tax collections remain approximately level." [#q0579] Tax levies consist of multiple components: operating levies for general funds, a debt service levy, and occasionally recoupment levies to recover revenues from prior years. [#q0579] For example, in 2024, the board approved residential rates of $3.8504 per $100 assessed valuation (including a $0.5110 debt service levy) and commercial rates of $4.7764 per $100 (including debt service and recoupment levies). [#q0106] The board may reduce tax rates without voter approval, but increasing rates beyond authorized ceilings requires voter authorization. [#q1268]

google/gemini-2.5-flash.answer: The Clayton School District Board of Education annually establishes property tax rates by October 1st, following a public hearing to gather comments [#q0579, #q0578, #q0577]. Louis County Assessor's Office and reviewed by the State Auditor's Office [#q0106, #q0579]. In September 2022, the board approved property tax rates of $4.0326 per $100 for residential real estate, $4.8014 per $100 for commercial real estate, and $4.5567 per $100 for personal property [#q0e72]. In September 2024, the board approved rates of $3.8504 per $100 for residential real estate (including a $0.5110 debt service levy), $4.7764 per $100 for commercial real estate (including a $0.5110 debt service levy and a $0.1300 recoupment levy), and $4.4447 per $100 for personal property (including a $0.5110 debt service levy) [#q0106]. Missouri's property tax system is designed so that when assessed valuations rise, rates are typically forced to decrease to keep tax collections relatively stable, and vice versa when valuations decline [#q0579, #q0578, #q0577].

openai/gpt-5.answer:
- The Board holds a public tax rate hearing before setting property tax rates, with the purpose to receive public comments, and rates must be set before October 1 under Missouri’s process; in 2025 staff noted the system adjusts rates as assessed valuations change and generally limits growth to the CPI, with data typically arriving mid-September, leaving a short window to adopt rates [#q0579] [#q0578].  
- In 2024, the Board held a public tax rate hearing where the CFO presented the proposed rates, and then approved the 2024 tax rates: $3.8504 per $100 for residential (including $0.5110 debt levy), $4.7764 for commercial (including $0.5110 debt levy plus $0.1300 recoupment), and $4.4447 for personal property (including $0.5110 debt levy) [#q010e] [#q0106].  
- The district’s audited data for FY2025 reflects those 2024-25 adjusted rates by subclass—$3.8504 residential, $4.7764 commercial, and $4.4447 personal property—and shows the 2025 total blended levy allocation by fund as General $1.5748, Special Revenue $1.8600, Capital Projects $0.3100, and Debt Service $0.5110 (per $100 of assessed valuation) [#q1ec4].  
- For context, in 2023 the Board approved property tax rates of $3.8772 residential, $4.6025 commercial, and $4.4447 personal property (per $100 of assessed valuation) [#q011e].  
- An earlier 2022 meeting shows the Board approving rates of $4.0326 residential, $4.8014 commercial, and $4.5567 personal property, and adjourning the tax rate hearing at 7:07 p.m. to enter the business meeting [#q0e72].  
- District budget materials explain that by September 30 each year the Board must establish a tax rate within state law and policy, can reduce rates without voter approval, and may revise the tax rate ceiling to compensate for assessed value reductions or to recoup prior-year revenue losses allowed by decisions of tax authorities [#q1268].

anthropic/claude-haiku-4.5.answer:
# Capital Projects Fund Spending

The Capital Projects Fund had $5.3 million in revenues and $5.6 million in expenditures for the year ended June 30, 2025, with the fund's balance decreasing $0.3 million before transfers. [#q1e69] Expenditures in the Capital Projects Fund increased by approximately $1.6 million in accordance with planned capital expenditures. [#q1e71] Looking back to fiscal year 2023, the Capital Projects Fund had $6.2 million in revenues and $7.0 million in expenditures, with the fund's balance decreasing $0.8 million before transfers. [#q1c4c] The increase in Capital Projects expenditures of approximately $2.3 million from the prior year was in accordance with planned expenditures for the safety and security plan and for the renovation of the Clayton High School library. [#q1c53] In the 2019-2020 proposed budget, the Capital Projects Fund was budgeted at $2.2 million, representing a $636,940 increase from the prior year. [#q126c] This increase was due to the successful passing of Proposition E, which allowed for an additional $100,000 toward capital projects and $575,000 toward the payment of the debt on the improvements to the Center of Clayton. [#q126c]

anthropic/claude-haiku-4.5.answer: For the 2021-2022 fiscal year, "Operation and Maintenance of Plant Services" was budgeted at $56,100 [#q1374], while related care and upkeep categories totaled significantly more: Building Services at $8,072,610, Grounds Services at $527,000, Equipment Services at $61,100, and Vehicle Servicing at $105,150 [#q1374]. In 2018-2019, Operation and Maintenance of Plant Services actual spending was $463,612, compared to $56,100 proposed for 2021-2022 [#q1374]. The largest single maintenance category, Care and Upkeep of Building Services, grew from $6,368,657 in 2018-2019 to $8,072,610 in the 2021-2022 budget [#q1374]. A maintenance facility location also appears in the budget, with $3,172,380 allocated for 2021-2022, including $1,435,220 in certified salaries, $229,750 in classified salaries, and $647,930 in retirement benefits [#q1365].

gpt-5-mini.answer: The district’s budget groups building- and grounds-related maintenance under several function codes, including Operation and Maintenance of Plant Services, Care and Upkeep of Building Services, Care and Upkeep of Grounds Services, Care and Upkeep of Equipment Services, and Vehicle Servicing and Maintenance. [#q1307] The most recent budgeted totals for 2021–22 show Care and Upkeep of Building Services at $8,072,610 and Operation and Maintenance of Plant Services at $56,100, with Grounds, Equipment, and Vehicle maintenance budgeted at $527,000, $61,100, and $105,150 respectively. [#q1374] In the 2021–22 location-level detail, the Maintenance location is budgeted $3,172,380 total (including $249,580 in capital/equipment), and capital/outlay for facilities appears across locations (for example Center of Clayton $533,440; Central Office $508,730). [#q1361] Year‑to‑year comparisons show increases from the 2020–21 proposed totals (e.g., total operating expenditures rising from $57,075,620 to $58,560,400), indicating higher overall allocations that include plant and maintenance functions. [#q1374]

gpt-5-mini+finance.answer: The district budgeted $11,824,429 for "Operation of plant" across all governmental funds in FY2024‑2025. [#q1e7a] The FY2023‑2024 amount was $10,531,268, an increase from $10,418,351 in FY2022‑2023. [#q1bf2] [#q1c5c] Earlier years show lower amounts: $8,749,742 in FY2021‑2022, $9,119,710 in FY2020‑2021, $8,314,700 in FY2019‑2020, and $7,554,122 in FY2018‑2019. [#q1ccb] [#q1d37] [#q1da4] [#q1e0a] Overall, the quoted figures indicate a general upward trend in operation of plant and facilities maintenance expenditures from FY2018‑2019 to FY2024‑2025, with the largest reported value in FY2024‑2025. [#q1e0a] [#q1e7a]

[#q1374] “Care and Upkeep of Grounds Services” is proposed at $527,000 in 2021–22, up from $471,600 in 2020–21. [#q1307] Location-level data for 2021–22 shows a Maintenance cost center totaling $3,172,380, including $1,435,220 in certified salaries, $229,750 in classified salaries, $421,640 in health benefits, $647,930 in retirement, $188,260 in purchased services, and $249,580 in capital equipment. [#q1365]

google/gemini-2.5-flash.answer: The Superintendent serves as the chief executive officer for the district and is employed by the Board of Education [#q125a, #q12b2, #q1387, #q13e1, #q1417, #q1320]. The Superintendent, along with the executive administrative team, makes recommendations and answers board questions [#q125a, #q12b2, #q1387, #q1320]. Nisha Patel, Ed.D. is currently listed as the Superintendent [#q1387, #q13e1, #q1417]. Previously, Sean Doherty, Ed.D. held the position of Superintendent [#q125a, #q12b2, #q1320]. In December 2020, discussions were held regarding a superintendent search, with 22 applications received and interviews planned for January 2021 [#q100d].

Stacy Siwak served as President [#q0124]. Stacy Siwak was elected President, Ms. Kim Hurst as Vice President, Ms. Chris Win as Secretary, and Mr. Jason Growe as Treasurer [#q000b]. google/gemini-2.5-flash.answer: Nisha Patel, Superintendent, served as temporary chair [#q1ee2]. Following his election, Mr. Growe facilitated the election of the Vice President, Secretary, and Treasurer [#q1ee2]. Chris Win was elected Vice President, Dr. Pamela Lyss-Lerman was elected Secretary, and Leo Human was elected Treasurer, all with unanimous "aye" votes after receiving single nominations for each position [#q1ee2]. Previously, in April 2024, Stacy Siwak was elected President, with Kim Hurst as Vice President, Chris Win as Secretary, and Jason Growe as Treasurer [#q000b]. Stacy Siwak was also listed as Board President in September 2024 minutes [#q0124] and in November 2024 minutes, where she announced a new board member and then signed off as Board President [#q00a8]. In April 2023, Amy Rubin was elected President, and Stacy Siwak was elected Vice President [#q0f4d]. Before that, on April 14, 2021, Amy Rubin was elected President and Gary Pierson was elected Vice President [#q1050].

anthropic/claude-haiku-4.5.answer: The Clayton School District Board of Education regularly approves meeting minutes through the consent agenda process. [#q1ee6] Most consent agenda items, including minutes approval, have gone through Board subcommittee review, and documentation is provided to all Board members and the public in advance for thorough review. [#q1ee6] Items may be removed from the consent agenda at any board member's request. [#q1ee6] Meeting minutes are consistently included as a consent agenda item across multiple board meetings. [#q1ee6] [#q00a2] [#q0012] [#q0091] [#q006b] [#q0057] [#q003c] [#q1060] The most recent example shows that on April 15, 2026, the board moved to approve the March 11, 2026 Business Meeting minutes as part of consent agenda item 7.3, with all board members voting aye. [#q1ee6]

google/gemini-2.5-flash.answer: The Clayton School District Board of Education frequently includes the approval of meeting minutes on its consent agenda [#q1ee6, #q0f12, #q00a2, #q0012, #q0091, #q006b, #q0057, #q003c, #q1edc, #q1060]. The consent agenda allows the Board to approve multiple items with a single motion, with items often having gone through subcommittee review [#q1ee6]. Any board member can request an item be removed from the consent agenda [#q1ee6]. For example, the April 15, 2026, meeting minutes show a motion to approve consent agenda items 7.2 through 7.3, which included personnel actions and approval of minutes from the March 11, 2026, business meeting, all of which carried with all aye votes [#q1ee6]. Similarly, the February 1, 2023, meeting minutes show the approval of consent agenda items 9.02 through 9.05, which included personnel and approval of minutes from January 18 and 19, 2023 [#q0f12].

gpt-5-mini.answer: Recent Clayton Board minutes show the board routinely approves meeting minutes as part of the consent agenda; April 15, 2026 minutes include an “Approval of Minutes” consent item for the March 11, 2026 business meeting and a motion to “approve the minutes as presented.” [#q1ee6] The board’s standard practice is to bundle approval of minutes with other consent items (personnel, financial reports, etc.) and approve them by a single motion covering multiple items. [#q1ee6] Earlier minutes show the same pattern: consent motions approving minutes appear repeatedly (for example, February 1, 2023; May 15, 2024; April 10, 2024; March 27, 2024; February 21, 2024; December 13, 2023; August 16, 2023; April 21, 2021), with motions carried. anthropic/claude-haiku-4.5.answer: Under Missouri law, public bodies may close meetings only to discuss matters directly related to an announced reason [#q11d5]. Permissible closed session topics include legal actions and confidential attorney communications [#q11cc]. The law requires that notices of closed meetings specify the statutory reason for closure, and only business directly related to that reason may be discussed [#q11a0].

Missouri Sunshine Law                                                                                                           
 6
Complaints and concerns regarding enforcement of Missouri’s Sunshine 
Law may be directed to the Missouri Attorney General’s Office or any local 
prosecuting attorney. 
MEETINGS OF PUBLIC GOVERNMENT BODIES
Meetings of public governmental bodies, where a quorum is present, and 
public business is discussed, are subject to the Sunshine Law. This includes 
telephone conferences and internet chat sessions, as well as meetings, or 
a series of meetings, each involving fewer than a quorum of the members,

google/gemini-2.5-flash.answer: For meetings closed to the public, only business directly related to the specific reason for closure may be discussed [#q11a0, #q11d5]. These reasons must be authorized by Section 610.021, RSMo, and stated in the notice for closed meetings [#q11a0, #q11b0]. One authorized reason for closing meetings, records, and votes is related to legal actions, causes of action, or litigation involving a public governmental body and communications with its attorneys [#q11cb, #q11cc]. Public bodies are not required to hold a closed meeting to discuss any matter [#q11d6].

gpt-5-mini.answer: The Sunshine Law permits public bodies to close meetings only for specific reasons listed in Section 610.021, RSMo., and allows closing meetings, records, and votes to the extent they relate to matters such as legal actions, causes of action, litigation, and confidential attorney communications. [#q11cb] The law requires that closed meetings be limited “only to the extent necessary for the specific reason announced” and that no business unrelated to that stated reason be discussed, with roll call votes required to close and recorded for all votes taken in closed session. [#q11d5] Notices for closed meetings must state the specific statutory reason under Section 610.021 that justifies closure and include the date, time, and place of the meeting. [#q11a0] Minutes or a journal of closed meetings must be kept, including date, time, place, members present and absent, and records of votes; some closed-record items (for example, minutes or settlement agreements tied to litigation) must be made public upon final disposition, though certain materials like legal work product remain closed. [#q11cb] The Sunshine Law also notes that attendees may not record closed meetings without permission. [#q119b] Finally, district records show examples of board adjournments into closed/executive session on specific meeting dates (e.g., September 23, 2020 and May 8, 2024). [#q0fb1] [#q1141]

gpt-5-mini.answer: The board has already taken the action to place a bond measure on the ballot and is in the campaign’s “solution phase,” focused now on informing voters about what is on the ballot and the specific needs the measure addresses leading up to the April 7 date. anthropic/claude-haiku-4.5.answer: At the December 2024 retreat, the board reviewed proposed design options and explored funding strategies [#q079e], with a focus on balancing improved facilities with fiscal responsibility [#q079d]. The January 2025 retreat continued this work, reviewing cost estimates for design options and refining them based on community feedback and financial constraints [#q079f]. Key concerns included how to prioritize projects within available funding limits and balance critical needs with fiscal constraints [#q079f]. A major priority identified through community engagement was "increasing and updating specialized learning spaces for real-world learning opportunities," which received 299 votes from respondents [#q079d]. The board's broader approach emphasized involving diverse stakeholders—parents, students, staff, and residents—to ensure their perspectives and needs were considered in identifying facility priorities [#q0781].

anthropic/claude-haiku-4.5.answer: The Clayton Board of Education welcomes public comments and has established a formal process for community members to participate [#q1452]. For electronic meetings, individuals must submit a form by 5 p.m. on the meeting date, including their name, address, email, and comments, with each comment limited to three minutes [#q1452]. The Board will not read comments addressing specific personnel issues; instead, such concerns should be directed through the District's established procedures [#q1452]. Public comments are read at the beginning of each meeting, and while the Board does not directly respond to speakers, the Board President may make clarifying remarks about any misstatements of fact, and the administration will send a written response to the speaker [#q1453].

google/gemini-2.5-flash.answer: The Board of Education welcomes public comments, especially during electronic meetings, and has established an alternative process for receiving them [#q1452, #q1453, #q1454]. Individuals wishing to comment during an electronic meeting must submit a form by 5 p.m. on the meeting day, including their name, address, email, and comments [#q1452, #q1455]. Each comment is typically allotted up to three minutes, though the Board President can adjust this time based on the number of comments [#q1452, #q1455]. Comments addressing specific personnel issues will not be read; such concerns should be handled through established District procedures for sharing concerns about school personnel [#q1452, #q1453, #q1455]. gpt-5-mini.answer: The Board has a written process for public comment during electronic meetings that requires speakers to submit a form by 5 p.m. on the day of the meeting that includes name, address, email and comments. [#q1452] Each submitted comment is allotted up to three minutes, though the Board president may change the time limit before the meeting to accommodate the number of comments. [#q1452] Comments addressing specific personnel matters will not be read and such concerns must be handled through the District’s established personnel procedures or brought to the Board privately. [#q1452] The Board generally does not directly respond during the meeting, but the Board President may make clarifying remarks at the end of the public comment session and the Administration will send a written response to the speaker. [#q1455] Recent minutes show occasions when no public comments were submitted or presented at meetings. [#q1f05]
